title: Faculty Identity, Vision, Mission, Values, Objectives
text:
Chapter One: Introduction.

a. About the Faculty:
Interest in data science has increased notably in recent times, from both corporations and individuals eager to learn these fields. Data science has become an interdisciplinary field that uses scientific methods, processes, algorithms, and systems to extract knowledge and insights from data in both structured and unstructured forms. According to recent reports, 90% of the data that exists today was created in the past two years. Consequently, data science is one of the most rapidly growing specializations, and demand for it will continue to increase due to the need of major global companies to analyze and handle the vast and ever-growing volume of data.

From this standpoint, Alexandria University decided to establish the Faculty of Computing and Data Science (approved by the University Council in its session on 31/3/2019) with unique specializations representing modern trends aligned with technological progress, serving the labor market and contributing to building the digital society, developing its information infrastructure, and acquiring skills in learning, research, and innovation. The bachelor's program at the faculty spans four academic years divided into eight semesters of fifteen weeks each under the credit-hour system. The faculty initially offers six distinguished programs: Computing and Data Sciences, Business Analytics, Intelligent Systems, Media Analytics, Healthcare Informatics and Data Analytics, and Cybersecurity, with the faculty team continuing to pursue development policies to achieve the faculty's objectives and serve the Egyptian business environment and society, as well as the possibility of opening new programs to meet these requirements.

b. Vision:
The faculty seeks to provide distinguished education and scientific research in the fields of computing and data science by offering modern academic programs in accordance with national and international quality-of-education standards, using advanced learning methods to create highly competitive specialists in computing and data science.

c. Mission:
Preparing distinguished and specialized cadres, and qualifying competencies that compete academically and professionally in the fields of computing and data science, by offering distinguished educational programs according to national and international quality-of-education standards, contributing to building the knowledge economy, solving society's technical and informational problems, and preparing graduates capable of enabling various labor-market sectors to leverage modern technology and information techniques to serve society.

d. Values:
1. Creativity and Innovation
2. Quality and Excellence
3. Teamwork and Team Spirit
4. Belonging and Commitment
5. Freedom of Thought
6. Justice and Equal Opportunity
7. Integrity and Transparency

e. Faculty Objectives:
1. Keeping pace with global academic and scientific development at international universities in computing and data science.
2. Enhancing learners' expertise so they possess high-level scientific and practical experience in their specializations.
3. Qualifying graduates and equipping them with cognitive skills to achieve competitiveness in computing and data science and contributing to community service.
4. Ensuring program coherence through the integration of courses representing the latest scientific and technical developments in the fields of specialization.
5. Merging scientific programs and building bridges between computer science and information technology.
6. Participating in providing consultancy, training, and educational programs in computing and data science, reinforcing the faculty's role in community service.
7. Establishing and stimulating scientific research among students and faculty members to contribute to sustainable community development.
8. Providing greater space for students for practical and laboratory training that keeps pace with work requirements across all state institutions.

title: Degrees Awarded
text: Article (1) Degrees Awarded: The university grants a Bachelor’s degree in Computers and Data Science in the following programs: Computing & Data Sciences (Code 01). Business Analytics (Code 02). Intelligent Systems (Code 03). Media Analytics (Code 04). Healthcare Informatics and Data Analytics (Code 05). Cybersecurity (Code 06).

title: Academic Departments
text: Article (2) Academic Departments: The faculty consists of two departments: Computing and Information Systems, and Data Sciences and Analytics.

title: General Admission Requirements
text: Article (3) General Admission Requirements: Subject to the Egyptian government university admission system. International students require approval from the General Administration for International Students and must pass admission tests.

title: Coding System
text: Article (4) Coding System: Each course is identified by a structured code in the format 02-24-PPPLL, where 02 represents the university, 24 represents the faculty, PPP represents the program code, and LL represents the course sequence within the level. Example: 02-24-05401 (E-health, Telehealth and Telemedicine).

title: Study System
text:
Article (5) Study System:
The Bachelor's degree in Computing and Data Science is based on the credit hour system with a semester-based academic calendar. The academic year is divided into two main semesters (Fall and Spring), each lasting fifteen (15) weeks. The Faculty Council may approve an optional Summer semester lasting eight (8) weeks, during which the weekly teaching hours for courses are doubled. Written and practical/applied examinations follow the teaching period.

title: Credit Hour Definition
text:
Article (6) Credit Hour Definition:
A credit hour is a unit of measurement to determine the weight of each course in a main semester. It is equivalent to:
- One (1) theoretical/lecture hour per week
- Two (2) hours of exercises or practical lessons per week
- Three (3) hours of applied training per week
- Four (4) hours of field training per week

title: Language of Study
text:
Article (7) Language of Study:
English is the official language of instruction at the Faculty. Some courses may be taught in Arabic with the approval of both the Faculty Council and the University Council.

title: Graduation Requirements
text:
Article (8) Graduation Requirements:
To obtain the Bachelor's degree in Computing and Data Science, a student must complete a minimum of 140 credit hours, including university, faculty, and programme requirements, in no fewer than 7 main semesters, with a minimum cumulative CGPA of 2.00 at graduation.

First: University Requirements (10 credit hours):
a. Compulsory Requirements (4 credit hours):
   1. Critical Thinking (2 Cr)
   2. Innovation & Entrepreneurship (2 Cr)
   3. Human Rights and Anti-Corruption* 
   4. Community Service and Environmental Development*
   5. Military Education*
   * Graduation requirement; hours do not count toward the required credit hours.

b. Elective Requirements (6 credit hours):
   Three courses (2 Cr each) from the elective list offered by other university faculties. Students cannot register for electives offered by their own faculty.

Second: Faculty Requirements (60 credit hours):
a. Compulsory Faculty Courses (48 Cr)
b. Elective Faculty Courses (12 Cr)

Third: Programme Requirements (70 credit hours):
a. Compulsory Programme Courses (58 Cr, including 4 Cr field training)
b. Elective Programme Courses (12 Cr)

title: Evaluation and Grading System
text:
Article (9) Evaluation and Grading System:
a. The maximum score for each course is 100 marks.
b. The Faculty Council determines the grade distribution for practical exams, term work, oral exams (if any), and the final written exam according to each course's nature as specified in the regulations. The final written exam ranges from 50 to 70 marks. Oral exam marks (if any) do not exceed 10 marks.
c. A student is considered to have failed (F) in a course if they score less than 30% in the final written exam, regardless of their total course marks.

d. Grading Scale:
   A   = 90% and above        → 4.000 points
   A-  = 85% to less than 90% → 3.666 points
   B+  = 80% to less than 85% → 3.333 points
   B   = 75% to less than 80% → 3.000 points
   B-  = 70% to less than 75% → 2.666 points
   C+  = 65% to less than 70% → 2.333 points
   C   = 60% to less than 65% → 2.000 points
   C-  = 56% to less than 60% → 1.666 points
   D+  = 53% to less than 56% → 1.333 points
   D   = 50% to less than 53% → 1.000 points
   F   = Less than 50% total   → 0.000 points
   (F) = Less than 30% in final written exam → 0.000 points
   Abs = Absent from final exam without accepted excuse → 0.000 points

e. Additional symbols (no points calculated):
   W   = Withdrawal (voluntary)
   FW  = Forced Withdrawal (exceeded absence limit)
   MW  = Military Withdrawal
   I   = Incomplete (did not complete course requirements)
   IP  = In-Progress (for courses spanning two semesters)
   S   = Satisfactory (for university compulsory requirements)
   U   = Unsatisfactory (for university compulsory requirements)
   AU  = Audit

f. Semester GPA = Sum of (Course Points × Credit Hours) / Total Credit Hours registered in semester.
   Cumulative CGPA = Sum of (Course Points × Credit Hours for all courses) / Total Credit Hours for all courses across all semesters.
   Both GPA and CGPA are rounded to four decimal places.

title: Academic Load
text:
Article (10) Academic Load:
a. The minimum number of credit hours a student can register for in Fall and Spring semesters is 12 credit hours, except for graduation cases based on the recommendation of the Education and Student Affairs Committee and approval of the Faculty Council.
b. The maximum credit hours in Fall and Spring semesters is 19 credit hours, which may be increased to 21 credit hours for students with a cumulative CGPA of at least 3.333 (see Article 9: Evaluation and Grading) or for graduating students in their final level who are not academically struggling.
c. Academically struggling students are not allowed to register for more than 12 credit hours in Fall and Spring semesters.
d. The maximum credit hours in the Summer semester is 9 credit hours.

title: Study Levels
text:
Article (11) Study Levels:
Students are classified into four academic levels based on the number of credit hours they have passed:
- Level 1: 0 to 28 credit hours
- Level 2: 29 to 64 credit hours
- Level 3: 65 to 98 credit hours
- Level 4: 99 to 140 credit hours

title: Academic Advisor
text:
Article (12) Academic Advisor:
The Education and Student Affairs Committee designates an academic advisor for each student to help the student understand their study plan, monitor its implementation, and select compulsory and elective courses according to that plan. The advisor's opinion is consultative and non-binding. However, course registration is not approved without the academic advisor's approval of the student's academic load.

title: Course Registration
text:
Article (13) Course Registration:
a. The faculty announces compulsory and elective courses to be offered at least one month before the start of each semester. Registration begins at least one week before the semester.
b. The Faculty Council may allow late registration during the second week for students who missed the registration period with an excuse accepted by the Education and Student Affairs Committee.
c. Students cannot register for any course that has a prerequisite unless they have passed that prerequisite. The Faculty Council sets the necessary controls for this.

title: Add and Drop
text:
Article (14) Add and Drop:
Students may add or drop courses from the beginning of the second week until the end of the third week of a main semester, or until the end of the first week of the Summer semester. This requires completing the add/drop form and obtaining approval from the academic advisor. Dropped courses do not appear on the student's academic record, and the add/drop must not violate the student's academic load requirements (see Article 10: Academic Load).

title: Course Withdrawal
text:
Article (15) Course Withdrawal:
a. Students may withdraw from a course from the fourth week until the end of the twelfth week of a main semester, and during the sixth week of the Summer semester. This requires completing the withdrawal form and obtaining approval from the academic advisor, provided the withdrawal does not violate the student's academic load (see Article 10). In this case, the course hours are not counted and a 'W' (Withdrawal) grade is recorded, provided the student has not exceeded the permitted absence limit before withdrawing.
b. Withdrawal requests after the specified deadlines are referred to the Education and Student Affairs Committee for review.

title: Attendance
text: Article (16) Attendance: Minimum 75% practical attendance. Warnings at 15% and 20% absence. At 25% absence, the student is barred and receives an "FW".

title: Missing Final Exams
text:
Article (17) Missing Final Exams:
a. If a student misses the final exam for any course and submits a force majeure excuse accepted by the Education and Student Affairs Committee within two (2) days of the exam date, and completes an Incomplete course request, they receive a temporary 'Incomplete (I)' grade, provided they have achieved at least 60% of the term work marks and have not been barred from taking the final exam (see Article 16: Attendance).
b. For Incomplete (I) courses, the student may take the final exam during the first week of the immediately following semester, or as determined by the Faculty Council. The total grade is the final exam score plus previously earned term work marks.
c. If a student fails to complete a course with an Incomplete (I) grade, they receive an 'Abs' grade and must re-study the course.
d. A student may not receive more than 3 Incomplete requests across all semesters during their study period.
e. If a student misses the final exam without an accepted excuse, they receive an 'Abs' grade and must re-study the course.

title: Course Repetition
text:
Article (18) Course Repetition:
a. If a student receives an F, (F), Abs, or FW grade in any course, they must re-study it if it is compulsory, or may take a substitute course if it is elective. The new grade is recorded.
b. If a student receives an Unsatisfactory (U) grade in a graduation requirement, they must re-study it.
c. Academically struggling students may repeat courses to improve their cumulative CGPA.
d. When calculating the cumulative CGPA, only the passing grade is counted for the first 8 course retakes. For retakes beyond 8, both failing and passing grades are counted in the CGPA.
e. If a student fails the same course multiple times, only one failure is counted in the CGPA, but the number of attempts is recorded in the academic transcript. Points from all attempts are included in the CGPA calculation across all semesters.

title: Academic Monitoring (Probation)
text:
Article (19) Academic Monitoring (Probation):
a. A student is considered academically struggling and is warned and placed under academic monitoring if their cumulative CGPA is less than 1.666 at the end of their second semester, or less than 2.000 in any subsequent semester.
b. The academic advisor monitors the struggling student's performance and requests placing them on Academic Probation for the following semester. The student is not allowed to register for more than 12 credit hours.
c. Students on the warning and monitoring list may repeat courses for improvement, and the higher grade is counted.
d. If a student on Academic Probation achieves a cumulative CGPA of 2.000 or higher in a semester, probation is lifted.
e. A student is dismissed from the faculty if their cumulative CGPA remains below 2.000 for three (3) consecutive semesters or four (4) non-consecutive semesters.

title: Suspension of Enrollment
text:
Article (20) Suspension of Enrollment:
a. A student may apply to suspend their enrollment for one or more main semesters, up to a maximum of four (4) main semesters (consecutive or non-consecutive), for reasons accepted by the Education and Student Affairs Committee and approved by the Faculty Council.
b. A student is considered to have withdrawn from the semester if they do not register for any course during that semester, and it counts toward the four suspension opportunities.
c. Students may withdraw from the academic programme after registration if called for military service, receiving a 'Military Withdrawal (MW)' grade. This withdrawal does not count toward the four permitted suspension opportunities.

title: Cancellation of Enrollment
text:
Article (21) Cancellation of Enrollment:
A student may cancel their enrollment in the programme at their request. They may also apply for re-enrollment within four (4) main semesters at most, providing reasons for both cancellation and re-enrollment. The application is reviewed by the Faculty Council, and if recommended for approval, it is presented to the University Council. If approved, the student is treated as a continuing student based on their previous status before cancellation. The student is not allowed to suspend enrollment after re-enrollment.

title: Graduation and Honors
text:
Article (22) Graduation and Honors:
a. The faculty allows graduation at three points in the academic year:
   - January session (after Fall semester)
   - June session (after Spring semester)
   - September session (after Summer semester)

b. Honors are awarded to students who complete graduation requirements within a maximum of 9 main semesters, provided:
   - Their semester GPA is at least 3.333 in every semester
   - Their cumulative CGPA is at least 3.666
   - They have never received F, (F), FW, or U grades in any course
   - They have not received any disciplinary penalties during their studies

c. In case of tied CGPA among top graduates in any graduation session, the CGPA is rounded to four decimal places and ranking is determined according to criteria set by the Faculty Council, University Council standards, and applicable laws and regulations.

title: Changing Programme
text:
Article (23) Changing Programme:
A student may change their academic programme after spending a period in the same faculty, provided they meet the admission requirements of the new programme, obtain the academic advisor's opinion, get approval from the Education and Student Affairs Committee, and the Faculty Council's endorsement. Courses previously passed that are part of the new programme's requirements may be credited. Other courses are not counted toward the new programme's credit hours.

title: Audit System
text:
Article (24) Audit System:
The Faculty Council may accept students from the same faculty, other faculties, or other universities to attend certain courses as auditors, according to rules set by the Faculty Council. The faculty issues an attendance certificate for auditing students.

title: Student Mobility (Exchange)
text:
Article (25) Student Mobility / Exchange:
a. A student may register for courses from outside the faculty, university, or from a foreign university, to be counted within their programme, after equivalence of course content is verified and the Faculty Council approves. These courses are included in the CGPA calculation. They may not exceed 25% of the total graduation credit hours.
b. Visiting students from outside the university may study courses at the faculty without obtaining a degree, with approval from the Education and Student Affairs Committee and according to Faculty Council rules. The maximum registration for a main semester is 12 credit hours and 9 for Summer. The student receives a certificate of courses studied and grades achieved.

title: Continuous Learning
text:
Article (26) Continuous Learning:
a. The Faculty Council, with University Council approval, may offer certain courses or course packages for graduates from the same faculty, other faculties, or other universities, or those at equivalent levels, according to Faculty Council rules approved by the University.
b. Maximum registration is 12 credit hours per main semester and 9 credit hours for Summer, with regulations from Article 12 through Article 17 applied.
c. At the end of the period, the learner receives a certificate showing courses studied, passed, and grades obtained according to Article 9: Evaluation and Grading.

title: Implementation of Regulations
text:
Article (27) Implementation of Regulations:
The Faculty Council has the authority to take whatever decisions are necessary to complete and implement these regulations and facilitate their procedures.

title: Core Competencies
text:
Article (28) Core Competencies of Academic Programs:
The Faculty of Computing and Data Science adopts a competency-based learning methodology. Based on the competencies required by graduates in the labor market, the faculty's various academic programs include competencies embedded in the National Skills Framework for ICT (adopted by the Ministry of Communications and Information Technology), as well as other reference competencies adopted by international programs in corresponding specializations. Competencies were selected based on labor market needs at the national level, the Middle East, and globally, as Egyptian educational institutions provide a large pool of highly qualified professionals in the ICT sector for the region and worldwide. Accordingly, the selection of academic programs is based on realistic and studied needs. Starting from the professions required in the ICT sector and the skills and competencies needed for them, these competencies guided the selection of this regulation's programs to ensure graduate suitability for labor market needs and equip them with the necessary tools. This requires delivering educational content through innovative, student-centered, and industry-linked methods.

The following core competencies have been identified for each program:

Business Analytics Competencies:
1. Analysis and modeling of business processes.
2. Innovating data collection solutions based on modern technology trends.
3. Building data warehouses from various data sources.
4. Using various data analysis methods to support business decisions.
5. Localizing and managing information systems, including the legal aspect, to support digital transformation.
6. Analyzing data in various sectors with high data generation capacity, such as digital marketing, e-commerce, and e-government services.
7. Developing solutions and products based on data analytics technology as a nucleus for new ventures.

Intelligent Systems Competencies:
1. Identifying, analyzing, and evaluating components and fundamentals of intelligent systems.
2. Collecting and analyzing information and predictions from big data and assisting in decision-making.
3. Designing, building, and testing intelligent computing systems.
4. Designing and building embedded systems and robotics software.
5. Understanding how to design, create, and manage smart cities.
6. Providing the necessary support, management, and maintenance to ensure the effectiveness and efficiency of intelligent systems.
7. Reviewing and evaluating available results to determine the usefulness and effectiveness of existing systems.

Media Analytics Competencies:
1. Analyzing media content and exploring changes in the digital media environment at local and global levels.
2. Producing all forms of digital radio and television programs using multimedia, digital photography, and editing techniques.
3. Using computing and technology to analyze data across all journalistic work elements (reports, articles, investigations) and even digital press design techniques.
4. Developing media practices by leveraging computing and data science expertise.
5. Localizing digitization in various media institutions (radio, press, television), including transition to digital archiving.
6. Building observatories for big data analysis on social networks and sentiment analysis.
7. Innovating mechanisms for public opinion surveys on various issues based on electronic measurement and electronic surveys to serve national objectives.
8. Innovating media policies based on scientific analyses of international media platforms, documentaries, and recordings.

Healthcare Informatics and Data Analytics Competencies:
1. Describing fundamental concepts in health, medical, and biomedical sciences.
2. Using data, information, and knowledge in various health fields.
3. Understanding key health-related determinants such as genes, physiology, psychology, and environment, and how to assess them.
4. Identifying mechanisms for clinical, diagnostic, and therapeutic health decision-making.
5. Enabling information sharing among different healthcare institutions.
6. Excelling in management, financing, and servicing of healthcare systems.
7. Using information to support public health and research serving health fields.

Cybersecurity Competencies:
1. Analyzing, designing, and building secure IT systems.
2. Providing the necessary support, management, and maintenance to ensure the effectiveness and efficiency of IT and security systems.
3. Identifying, analyzing, and mitigating threats to IT systems or networks.
4. Investigating cyber events/IT crimes and systems, networks, and digital evidence.
5. Collecting and analyzing cybersecurity information that may be used to develop protection of systems and networks.
6. Analyzing, reviewing, and evaluating available cybersecurity information to determine its usefulness for investigations.
7. Providing leadership, management, and guidance for individuals and organizations to act effectively and with cybersecurity awareness.

title: Programme Design
text:
Article (29) Programme Design:
The 140 total credit hours are distributed as follows:
- University Requirements: 10 credit hours
- Faculty Requirements: 60 credit hours (48 compulsory + 12 elective)
- Programme Requirements: 70 credit hours (58 compulsory incl. 4 Cr field training + 12 elective)

The Faculty offers the following programmes:
1. Computing and Data Sciences (General Programme)
2. Business Analytics
3. Intelligent Systems
4. Media Analytics
5. Healthcare Informatics and Data Analytics
6. Cybersecurity

title: Faculty Compulsory (48 Cr)
text: Article (30) Faculty Compulsory Courses (48 Credit Hours): The faculty compulsory courses consist of sixteen (16) courses totaling forty-eight (48) credit hours. These courses include: 02-24-00101 Linear Algebra, 02-24-00102 Calculus, 02-24-00103 Introduction to Computer Systems, 02-24-00104 Introduction to Data Sciences, 02-24-00105 Programming I, 02-24-00106 Probability and Statistics I, 02-24-00107 Discrete Structures, 02-24-00108 Data Structures and Algorithms, 02-24-00109 Introduction to Artificial Intelligence, 02-24-00110 Programming II, 02-24-00201 Probability and Statistics II, 02-24-00202 Introduction to Databases, 02-24-00203 Numerical Computations, 02-24-00204 Cloud Computing, 02-24-00205 Machine Learning, 02-24-00206 Data Mining and Analytics.

text:

title: Content of Compulsory Faculty Courses
text: Article (44) Content of Compulsory Faculty Courses: The scientific content of the compulsory faculty courses is detailed below for each course code, including the main topics and subject areas covered.

title: Linear Algebra
text: 02-24-00101 Linear Algebra (3 Cr): Systems of linear equations, Matrices, Determinants, Euclidean spaces, Linear combinations and linear span, Subspaces, Linear independence, Basis and dimension, Rank of a matrix, Inner products, Eigenvalues, and Eigenvectors.

title: Calculus
text: 02-24-00102 Calculus (3 Cr): Functions and models, Limits and derivatives, Differentiation rules, Applications of differentiation, Integrals and applications of integration, Techniques of integration, Differential equations, Partial differential equations.

title: Introduction to Computer Systems
text: 02-24-00103 Introduction to Computer Systems (3 Cr): Introduction to computer systems, Representation and manipulation of information, Machine level representation of programs, Introduction to computer organization, Memory hierarchy, System I/O, Introduction to computer networks.

title: Introduction to Data Sciences
text: 02-24-00104 Introduction to Data Sciences (3 Cr): Introduction to data science, Data science ecosystem, Data representation and manipulation, Tools for data scientists, Data analytics tracks, Case studies using R.

title: Programming I
text: 02-24-00105 Programming I (3 Cr): Introduction to programming, Basic programming constructs, Branching and Iteration, Decomposition, Abstractions and Functions, Recursion, Structure types, Mutability, High order functions, Testing and debugging.

title: Probability and Statistics I
text: 02-24-00106 Probability and Statistics I (3 Cr): Sets, Techniques of counting, Probability spaces, Independence and dependence, Conditional probabilities, Random variables, Expectation, variance, and moments, Moment generating functions, Discrete and continuous distributions, Joint and marginal distributions.

title: Discrete Structures
text: 02-24-00107 Discrete Structures (3 Cr): Sequences and Summations, Growth of functions, Logic and Predicates, Proof techniques, Recursive relations, Advanced counting techniques, Functions and Relations, Graph and Tree structures, Introduction to number theory (Groups, Rings, Fields).

title: Data Structures and Algorithms
text: 02-24-00108 Data Structures and Algorithms (3 Cr): Arrays, Linear lists, Queues, Stacks, Tree structures and traversals, Dictionaries, Search trees, Heaps, Tries, Sorting and Searching, Hashing, Basic graph algorithms (Traversals, MST, Shortest Paths).

title: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
text: 02-24-00109 Introduction to Artificial Intelligence (3 Cr): Reasoning, Intelligent agents, Knowledge representation, Searching, Constraint satisfaction, Logic programming, Uncertain knowledge, Probabilistic reasoning, Planning, Applications.

title: Programming II
text: 02-24-00110 Programming II (3 Cr): Object-oriented concepts (Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism), UML, Requirement analysis, Object-oriented design, Operator overloading, Genericity, Thread programming, APIs, Software design patterns.

title: Probability and Statistics II
text: 02-24-00201 Probability and Statistics II (3 Cr): Normal distribution, Law of large numbers, Central limit theorem, Chi-squared, Student-t, and F distributions, Statistical estimation, Test of hypotheses, Fitting straight lines, ANOVA, Stochastic models, Poisson processes.

title: Introduction to Databases
text: 02-24-00202 Introduction to Databases (3 Cr): Database concepts, Data modeling, Relational model, Relational algebra, Normalization, Query languages/optimization, B trees, Transaction processing, Concurrency control, Recovery.

title: Numerical Computations
text: 02-24-00203 Numerical Computations (3 Cr): Matrix manipulation, Simultaneous linear equations, Approximation of roots, Error analysis, Numerical differentiation/integration, Interpolation/Extrapolation, Least-square approximation, Differential equations.

title: Cloud Computing
text: 02-24-00204 Cloud Computing (3 Cr): Introduction to Cloud, Cloud platforms/infrastructure, Parallel programming in the Cloud, Distributed storage systems, Virtualization, Cloud security, Cloud performance.

title: Machine Learning
text: 02-24-00205 Machine Learning (3 Cr): Feature representation, Similarity, Dimensionality reduction, Supervised learning, Regression, Unsupervised learning, Evaluation, Fundamentals of neural networks (Feedforward/Backpropagation), Reinforcement learning.

title: Data Mining and Analytics
text: 02-24-00206 Data Mining and Analytics (3 Cr): Data exploration/visualization, Preprocessing, Classification (SVM, Bayesian networks), Mining frequent patterns, Clustering, Text mining, Current trends.

title: Content of Faculty Elective Courses
text: Article (45) Content of Faculty Elective Courses: The scientific content of the faculty elective courses is detailed below for each listed course.

title: Software Engineering
text: 02-24-00301 Software Engineering (3 Cr): Lifecycle models, Project management, Requirements engineering, Modeling, Design techniques, Coding, Verification/Validation, Evolution, Reliability, Formal methods.

title: Systems Analysis and Design
text: 02-24-00302 Systems Analysis and Design (3 Cr): Analysis and design tools, Advanced design concepts, Case studies, Practical projects, Current development trends.

title: Algorithm Design
text: 02-24-00303 Algorithm Design (3 Cr): Asymptotic notations, Amortized analysis, Randomization, Divide-and-conquer, Dynamic programming, Greedy methods, String/Geometric/Number-theoretic algorithms, Complexity classes (NP-complete), Approximation algorithms.

title: Distributed Processing
text: 02-24-00304 Distributed Processing (3 Cr): Architecture models, Distributed communication (RPC, RMI, MPI), Naming, Distributed file systems (GFS, NFS), Synchronization, Fault tolerance, Consistency models, Paxos, MapReduce.

title: Mobile Programming
text: 02-24-00305 Mobile Programming (3 Cr): Frameworks, Design techniques, Android (Java), iOS (Swift), Native React, RESTful apps, Cloud services, Sensors, Security, Privacy, Usability.

title: Web Programming
text: 02-24-00306 Web Programming (3 Cr): Web fundamentals, HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Front-end programming, PHP, Django, AngularJS, React, Responsive design, Full stack state management, Security pitfalls.

title: Operating Systems
text: 02-24-00307 Operating Systems (3 Cr): Principles and structure, Processes/Threads, Synchronization, Scheduling, Memory management, Virtual memory, File systems, Virtual machines, Distributed OS, Case studies.

title: Computer Networks
text: 02-24-00308 Computer Networks (3 Cr): Architecture, Layering, Protocols, Socket programming, Transport layer (TCP), Reliable delivery (congestion/flow control), Network layer addressing, Routing algorithms.

title: Compulsory Content for Computing and Data Sciences
text: Article (46) Compulsory Content for Computing and Data Sciences: The scientific content of the compulsory courses specific to the Computing and Data Sciences program is detailed below.

title: Advanced Calculus
text: 02-24-01201 Advanced Calculus (3 Cr): Sequences, Series (Taylor's), Vector algebra, Functions of several variables, Partial derivatives, Gradients, Lagrange's multiplier, Multiple integrals.

title: Data Science Methodology
text: 02-24-01202 Data Science Methodology (3 Cr): Framework, Business understanding, RFM modelling, Structured/Unstructured data, Cleaning/Exploration, Market basket analysis, Modeling, Evaluation.

title: Stochastic Processes
text: 02-24-01301 Stochastic Processes (3 Cr): Discrete-time Markov chains, Poisson process, Continuous time Markov chain, Birth and death processes, Martingale, Queuing models/theory.

title: Big Data Analytics
text: 02-24-01401 Big Data Analytics (3 Cr): Issues, Processing, Streaming, Web/Social networks data, Classification/Clustering of big data, Predicting using models, Scaling traditional techniques.

title: Stream Processing
text: 02-24-01407 Stream Processing (3 Cr): Big data online, Implications, Applications, Stream processing frameworks/algorithms, Streaming SQL languages, Analytics while streaming.

title: Elective Content for Computing and Data Sciences
text: Article (47): Elective Content for Computing and Data Sciences. Includes content for 02-24-01409 through 02-24-01416.

title: Convex Optimization
text: 02-24-01409 Convex Optimization (3 Cr): Introduction to convex functions and sub-gradients, Gradient and sub-gradient methods, Accelerated proximal gradient methods, Lagrangian duals, and splitting algorithms.

title: Non-Linear and Combinatorial Optimization
text: 02-24-01410 Non-Linear and Combinatorial Optimization (3 Cr): Matching, Unimodular matrices, Matroids, Max-flows and min-cuts, Multicommodity flows, and heuristic techniques for hard optimization problems.

title: Multivariate Statistical Analysis
text: 02-24-01411 Multivariate Statistical Analysis (3 Cr): Distribution theory, Hotelling's T2 and Wishart distributions, Inference on mean and covariance, Principal components, and canonical correlation.

title: Bayesian Statistics
text: 02-24-01412 Bayesian Statistics (3 Cr): Bayes' theorem, prior distributions, likelihood, predictive distributions, numerical approximation, posterior simulation, and Markov chain simulation.

title: Data Compression Techniques
text: 02-24-01413 Data Compression Techniques (3 Cr): Information theory, Lossless/lossy compression, Huffman coding, LZW coding, Entropy coding, and applications to DNA sequences and multimedia.

title: Concurrent Algorithms and Data Structures
text: 02-24-01414 Concurrent Algorithms and Data Structures (3 Cr): Mutual exclusion, Shared memory foundations, Synchronization methods, and implementing concurrent datatypes like linked lists and skip lists.

title: Distributed Database Systems
text: 02-24-01415 Distributed Database Systems (3 Cr): Architectures, distributed data storage/indexing, parallel query processing/optimization, and concurrency control.

title: Advanced Database Systems
text: 02-24-01416 Advanced Database Systems (3 Cr): Transaction handling, embedded SQL, Triggers and stored procedures, and advanced structures in relational and object-oriented databases.

title: Compulsory Content for Business Analytics
text: Article (48): Compulsory Content for Business Analytics. Includes content for 02-24-02201, 02-24-02303, 02-24-02401.

title: Introduction to Business
text: 02-24-02201 Introduction to Business (3 Cr): Terminology, Concepts, Current practices, Business vocabulary, Analytical thinking, Decision-making skills.

title: Data Warehousing & Business Intelligence
text: 02-24-02303 Data Warehousing & Business Intelligence (3 Cr): Descriptive analysis, Integration, Hadoop framework, Multidimensional Design, ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes.

title: Leadership and People Analytics
text: 02-24-02401 Leadership and People Analytics (3 Cr): Theory of people analytics, Data-driven approach to management, Business leader decision-making based on deep analysis.

title: Elective Content for Business Analytics
text: Article (49): Elective Content for Business Analytics. Includes content for 02-24-02409 through 02-24-02418.

title: Human Computer Interaction
text: 02-24-02409 Human Computer Interaction (3 Cr): HCI principles, design methods for ERP systems, organizational decision support, and cognitive psychology in business systems.

title: Gamification and Games Development
text: 02-24-02410 Gamification and Games Development (3 Cr): Designing gamified experiences in real life, game design management, and engagement principles.

title: Technology Trends and Innovation
text: 02-24-02411 Technology Trends and Innovation (3 Cr): Perspective of a Chief Information Officer (CIO), IT investments, vendor management, and digital transformation.

title: GIS and Spatial Data Mining
text: 02-24-02412 GIS and Spatial Data Mining (3 Cr): Creation of spatial databases and identifying trends and patterns to extract hidden predictive information.

title: Managing Technology Projects
text: 02-24-02413 Managing Technology Projects (3 Cr): Planning, controlling, executing, and closing IT projects, specifically designed for novice or experienced IT managers.

title: Smart Cities and E-Government
text: 02-24-02414 Smart Cities and E-Government (3 Cr): Foundational elements of smart cities, e-Governance models, and the potential for IT to improve human-city interactions.

title: Digital Transformation and Digital Economics
text: 02-24-02415 Digital Transformation and Digital Economics (3 Cr): Sharing economy, value creation models, digital business models, and transformation of real economy into digital.

title: Manufacturing Analytics
text: 02-24-02416 Manufacturing Analytics (3 Cr): Analyzing big data in manufacturing and the role of the Internet of Things (IoT) for connected factories.

title: Predictive Analytics
text: 02-24-02417 Predictive Analytics (3 Cr): Statistical techniques for categorical responses and managerial decision-making in large data sets.

title: NLP and Semantic Analysis
text: 02-24-02418 NLP and Semantic Analysis (3 Cr): Algorithms for speech understanding, machine translation, and information extraction.

title: Compulsory Content for Intelligent Systems
text: Article (50): Compulsory Content for Intelligent Systems. Includes content for 02-24-03201, 02-24-03302, 02-24-03406.

title: Smart Systems and Computational Intelligence
text: 02-24-03201 Smart Systems and Computational Intelligence (3 Cr): Evolutionary algorithms, Genetic algorithms, Swarm Intelligence, Colony optimization, Fuzzy logic, Neuro-Fuzzy networks.

title: Deep Learning
text: 02-24-03302 Deep Learning (3 Cr): Auto-encoders, CNNs, RNNs, Deep Neural Networks, GANs, Deep reinforcement learning, Tuning, Regularization.

title: AI for Robotics
text: 02-24-03406 AI for Robotics (3 Cr): Kinematics, Transformations, Forward/Inverse kinematics, Jacobians, Programming on robot arms, Localization/Mapping, Kalman filters.

title: Elective Content for Intelligent Systems
text: Article (51): Elective Content for Intelligent Systems. Includes content for 02-24-03409, 02-24-03410, 02-24-03411, 02-24-03413, 02-24-03414, 02-24-03415, 02-24-03416.

title: Speech Recognition
text: 02-24-03409 Speech Recognition (3 Cr): Acoustic phonetics, Hidden Markov models, Viterbi algorithm, GMM acoustic modeling, and neural network recognition.

title: Natural Language Understanding
text: 02-24-03410 Natural Language Understanding (3 Cr): Lexical semantics, Relation extraction, Semantic Parsing, Sentiment analysis, and Dialogue agents.

title: Embedded Machine Learning
text: 02-24-03411 Embedded Machine Learning (3 Cr): Model compression and pruning, sensor data properties, wearable devices, and fitness/security applications.

title: Internet of Things II
text: 02-24-03413 Internet of Things II (3 Cr): Interoperability, sustainability data in cloud-based M2M systems, Edge Computing, and intelligent transportation systems.

title: Knowledge-Base AI
text: 02-24-03414 Knowledge-Base AI (3 Cr): Cognitive systems, semantic networks, Means-ends analysis, Analogical reasoning, and Visuospatial reasoning.

title: Virtual Reality
text: 02-24-03415 Virtual Reality (3 Cr): Architecture of 3D VR environments, Pipeline development, pixel shading, and lighting systems.

title: Game Theory
text: 02-24-03416 Game Theory (3 Cr): Normal form, Payoffs, Nash equilibria, zero-sum games, and imperfect-information games.

title: Compulsory Content for Media Analytics
text: Article (52): Compulsory Content for Media Analytics. Includes content for 02-24-04201, 02-24-04203, 02-24-04301, 02-24-04303, 02-24-04305.

title: Data Driven Journalism
text: 02-24-04201 Data Driven Journalism (3 Cr): Using big datasets to discover stories and public data usage in newsrooms.

title: Digital Video Production
text: 02-24-04203 Digital Video Production (3 Cr): Techniques of shooting, lighting, editing, storyboarding, and motion graphics software.

title: Image Processing
text: 02-24-04301 Image Processing (3 Cr): Acquisition, formation, enhancement, segmentation, and frequency domain linear filtering.

title: Computer Audio
text: 02-24-04303 Computer Audio (3 Cr): Acoustics and psychoacoustics, digital signal processing, and music theory for programmers.

title: Natural Language Processing
text: 02-24-04305 Natural Language Processing (3 Cr): Computational linguistics, statistical/neural-network learning algorithms, and text corpora.

title: Elective Content for Media Analytics
text: Article (53): Elective Content for Media Analytics. Includes content for 02-24-04409, 02-24-04411, 02-24-04413, 02-24-04415.

title: Interactive Media
text: 02-24-04409 Interactive Media (3 Cr): Cinematic, literary, artistic, and virtual interactive avenues.

title: Computational Photography
text: 02-24-04411 Computational Photography (3 Cr): 3D scanning, coded photography, light field imaging, and VR/AR displays.

title: Video Game Design and Programming
text: 02-24-04413 Video Game Design and Programming (3 Cr): Math, physics, level design, and problem-solving in functional game creation.

title: Digital Media Forensics
text: 02-24-04415 Digital Media Forensics (3 Cr): Computer crime, digital evidence, technological investigations, and expert witness skills.

title: Compulsory Content for Healthcare Informatics
text: Article (54): Compulsory Content for Healthcare Informatics. Includes content for 02-24-05201, 02-24-05202, 02-24-05301, 02-24-05401.

title: Introduction to Epidemiology
text: 02-24-05201 Introduction to Epidemiology (3 Cr): Concepts and methods applied to infectious and chronic diseases.

title: Anatomy & Physiology
text: 02-24-05202 Anatomy & Physiology (3 Cr): Structure and function of human body systems and anatomical terminology.

title: Neuroscience and Robotics
text: 02-24-05301 Neuroscience and Robotics (3 Cr): Integration of human brain functions with robotic computational perspectives.

title: E-health, Telehealth and Telemedicine
text: 02-24-05401 E-health, Telehealth and Telemedicine (3 Cr): Clinical and non-clinical telehealth applications and technical standards.

title: Elective Content for Healthcare Informatics
text: Article (55): Elective Content for Healthcare Informatics. Includes content for 02-24-05409 through 02-24-05413.

title: Radiation Physics
text: 02-24-05409 Radiation Physics (3 Cr): Radioactivity, dosimetry, imaging, and radiotherapy equipment.

title: Cellular & Molecular Biology
text: 02-24-05410 Cellular & Molecular Biology (3 Cr): Signal transduction, cell cycle, cancer biology, and human genome directed toward bioinformatics.

title: Radiation Biology
text: 02-24-05411 Radiation Biology (3 Cr): Linear energy transfer, tissue radiosensitivity, and radiobiological modelling.

title: Pathophysiology & Lab Data
text: 02-24-05412 Pathophysiology & Lab Data (3 Cr): Health and disease processes and interpretation of laboratory investigations.

title: Principles of Biochemistry
text: 02-24-05413 Principles of Biochemistry (3 Cr): Chemical processes of body metabolism and their reflection on health and disease.

title: Compulsory Content for Cybersecurity
text: Article (56): Compulsory Content for Cybersecurity. Includes content for 02-24-06201, 02-24-06203, 02-24-06406.

title: Introduction to Cybersecurity
text: 02-24-06201 Introduction to Cybersecurity (3 Cr): Need for security, Legal/Ethical issues, Risk management, Security technologies, Wireless security, Overview of Cryptography.

title: Cryptography
text: 02-24-06203 Cryptography (3 Cr): Encryption/Decryption, Data integrity, Attack classification, Symmetric/Asymmetric Ciphers, Digital Signatures, Zero-knowledge protocols.

title: Digital Forensics
text: 02-24-06406 Digital Forensics (3 Cr): Tools, Investigatory process, Acquisition/Preservation of evidence, Analysis techniques, Presentation of results, Mobile forensics.

title: Elective Content for Cybersecurity
text: Article (57): Elective Content for Cybersecurity. Includes content for 02-24-06409, 02-24-06411, 02-24-06412, 02-24-06413, 02-24-06416.

title: AI Security Issues
text: 02-24-06409 AI Security Issues (3 Cr): Trapdoors, malicious attacks for AI systems, and smart attacks.

title: Software Security Engineering
text: 02-24-06411 Software Security Engineering (3 Cr): SQL injection, cross-site scripting, Clickjacking, and session management.

title: Blockchain and Security of Blockchain
text: 02-24-06412 Blockchain and Security of Blockchain (3 Cr): Distributed ledger technologies, consensus mechanisms, and smart contracts.

title: Cloud Computing Security
text: 02-24-06413 Cloud Computing Security (3 Cr): Virtualization threats, identity management, Denial of Service, and secure APIs.

title: Mobile Computing
text: 02-24-06416 Mobile Computing (3 Cr): Wireless communication technologies, mobile user interfacing, and sensors programming.

title: Common First Year Study Plan
text: All six programs (Computing & Data Sciences, Business Analytics, Intelligent Systems, Media Analytics, Healthcare Informatics and Data Analytics, Cybersecurity) share the same first-year courses in Semesters 1 and 2. The first-year courses are listed under the Computing and Data Sciences program (Program 01). Semester 1 (17 Credits): 02-24-00101 Linear Algebra (3), 02-24-00102 Calculus (3), 02-24-00103 Introduction to Computer Systems (3), 02-24-00104 Introduction to Data Sciences (3), 02-24-00105 Programming I (3), 0200000XX Critical Thinking (2). Semester 2 (17 Credits): 02-24-00106 Probability and Statistics I (3), 02-24-00107 Discrete Structures (3), 02-24-00108 Data Structures and Algorithms (3), 02-24-00109 Introduction to Artificial Intelligence (3), 02-24-00110 Programming II (3), 0200000XX Innovation & Entrepreneurship (2).
